title: [BUG] Outdated dependencies causing deprecation warnings in Jenkins build (Node.js 24 + npm 11)
author: Ramya2801
body:
## рҹҗӣ Bug Report

ExcelJS 4.4.0 depends on deprecated packages that produce warnings during npm ci / npm install, creating noise in CI/CD pipelines and flagging in security review processes.

Lib version: 4.4.0 (latest as of 5 May 2026)

## Steps To Reproduce

```json
// package.json
{ "dependencies": { "exceljs": "^4.4.0" } }
```

```bash
# Install with Node 24 + npm 11
npm ci
```

### Output

```bash
npm warn deprecated rimraf@2.7.1: Rimraf versions prior to v4 are no longer supported
npm warn deprecated inflight@1.0.6: This module is not supported, and leaks memory. Do not use it.
npm warn deprecated glob@7.2.3: Old versions of glob are not supported, and contain widely publicized security vulnerabilities
npm warn deprecated fstream@1.0.12: This package is no longer supported.
npm warn deprecated glob@10.5.0: Old versions of glob are not supported
```

### Dependency chain

```bash
exceljs@4.4.0
в”ңв”Җв”Җ archiver@5.3.2
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ glob@7.2.3
в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җ inflight@1.0.6
в”ңв”Җв”Җ rimraf@2.7.1
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ glob@7.2.3 (duplicate)
в””в”Җв”Җ unzipper@0.10.14
    в””в”Җв”Җ fstream@1.0.12
```

## The expected behaviour:

npm ci should complete without deprecation warnings from transitive dependencies. All internal dependencies should use actively maintained packages.

comments.author: gameroman
comments.body:
Try one of these which are more actively maintained forks

https://github.com/excel-js/exceljs

https://github.com/cjnoname/excelts

https://github.com/protobi/exceljs

comments.author: uriah-chs
comments.body: Is anybody actively maintaining fork that is published to npm?

comments.author: gameroman
comments.body:
> Is anybody actively maintaining fork that is published to npm?

@uriah-chs see the comment above you

comments.author: protobi-pieter
comments.body:
I believe [exceljs](https://github.com/excel-js/exceljs) is not actively maintained.

we actively maintain https://github.com/protobi/exceljs  for our use, and it is a close descendent of excel-js/exceljs with many bug/security fixes.

https://github.com/cjnoname/excelts is a more radical fork -- all the js code was transpiled into typescript, and has been extensively extended with new features.

comments.author: uriah-chs
comments.body:
It seems the temporary fork might be permanent? :)  And "we" in this case is protobi?  This seems like maybe our best path forward.

The website listed on your GH profile "http://www.protobi.com/" seems to throwing Internal Server error sporadically when I was trying to hit it just FYI. Could have been cold start? seems to be working now anyway.

comments.author: gameroman
comments.body: You can also see a comparison on https://npmx.dev/compare?packages=exceljs,@cj-tech-master/excelts,@excel.js/exceljs,@protobi/exceljs

comments.author: uriah-chs
comments.body: @protobi-pieter , were you aware that your package still had the uuid vuln?

comments.author: gameroman
comments.body:
> [@protobi-pieter](https://github.com/protobi-pieter) , were you aware that your package still had the uuid vuln?

I opened https://github.com/protobi/exceljs/pull/81 but that will require a new major version

comments.author: protobi-pieter
comments.body:
@uriah-chs   What is the concern, is it more than `npm audit` shows a notice?

There is essentially no practical attack vector вҖ” for anyone, not just exceljs.   And the vulnerable code path is unreachable through Exceljs's API.  

The reported issue affects only **uuid** v3/v5/v6 (not v4) and even then you'd need to pass certain arguments that we don't pass.  And even  the "exploit" is just silently get a truncated/garbage UUID instead of a thrown error.  

ExcelJS uses only uuidv4() with no arguments :

  `{${uuidv4()}}`.toUpperCase()

- Uses v4 (not v3/v5/v6)
- No buf parameter
- No offset parameter
- No attacker-controlled input near the call

comments.author: uriah-chs
comments.body: a different third party vulnerability scanner, but yes. just want a clean bill of health if possible.  getting a security exception is extra work.
